comment: First Phoenix/Elixir bench run for the Parallax demo, captured 2026-05-30 on Apple M5 Pro (18-core), macOS 26.4.1, Elixir 1.19.5 + Erlang/OTP 29 [jit], Phoenix 1.8.7 with Bandit 1.11.1 adapter, wrk 4.2.0. Mirrors bench.sh's invocation shape exactly: cold-start = `wrk -t1 -c1 -d1s --latency`; steady-state per (impl, conn) = three rounds of `wrk -t4 -c<conn> -d10s --latency`. The req/s figures below are median across the three rounds with [min..max] range, matching the format used in the existing comparators' README.md.
machine: Apple M5 Pro (10P + 8E cores, 18 logical CPUs), 64 GB RAM, macOS 26.4.1

steady_state.notes: Two of three rounds suppressed the latency distribution (wrk timeouts dominated — bandit accept queue can't keep up at -c1000); req/s reported across all three rounds.

aggregated.note: Only one round produced a full latency distribution (round 3, ~165 req/s under timeouts); p99 from that round was 1300 ms (wrk's default 2s timeout ceiling clips higher values).

steady_state.notes: Bandit's listener can't accept 5000 concurrent inbound TCP connections within the 10s window; rounds 2-3 saw zero requests complete (read errors on the wrk side). One round limped through 33 requests. Phoenix is honest about its connection-acceptance ceiling at this conn count — same shape as Node at -c1000/5000 in the existing README. Tracked as a follow-up: tuning `:bandit_options.thousand_island_options.num_acceptors` (default ~10) might lift this.

aggregated.note: Two of three rounds produced zero completed requests.
